title: Melt-and-pour lavender soap
subtitle: No lye, no cure, bars ready the same afternoon
excerpt: A melt-and-pour lavender soap that skips lye work entirely. The glycerin base melts in a double boiler, takes lavender essential oil and dried flowers, then sets in two hours. Six 80 g guest bars from one 500 g block.
sourceNotes: Anne Watson, Smart Soapmaking (2007), Ch. 1, melt-and-pour overview. Bramble Berry melt-and-pour base formulation notes (brambleberry.com, accessed May 2026). Essential Oil Safety, Robert Tisserand and Rodney Young (2014), p. 327, lavender dermal-use limits for cosmetics.

content.text: A melt-and-pour lavender soap uses a pre-made glycerin base. There is no lye to measure and no cure to wait for. The base is already finished soap from the factory. Your session is heat, scent, colour, and mould. Six 80 g guest bars from one 500 g block, set within two hours.

The trade-off against cold-process is customisation. The base oils are fixed by the manufacturer; you can layer fragrances and botanicals, but you cannot change the oil profile or the superfat. For a first soap project or for making bars on the day, the melt-and-pour route is the right starting point.

items.name: Melt-and-pour soap base
items.prepNote: a clear or white glycerin base block. The clear base gives translucent bars; the white opaque base gives a classic white bar. Either works with lavender.

items.name: Lavender essential oil
items.prepNote: 2% of the base weight. Lavandula angustifolia or lavandin both work; add when the base is below 55°C so the top notes don't volatilise off.

content.text: Cut the base and melt gently

Cut the 500 g block into roughly 2 cm cubes with a sharp knife. The smaller the pieces, the more evenly they melt. Place the cubes in the top of a double boiler or in a heatproof bowl set over a saucepan of barely simmering water. Keep the heat low; the base melts between 50-65°C, and overheating above 75°C discolours the base and cooks off the fragrance before you've had a chance to add it.

Stir gently as the cubes soften. Avoid whipping in air bubbles, slow stirring with the silicone spatula keeps the surface smooth. When the last solid pieces have melted and the base is clear and liquid, check the temperature on the probe. The aim is 55-60°C for fragrance addition.

Remove the bowl from the heat. When the probe reads 55°C or below, measure 10 ml of lavender essential oil and stir through the melted base. Stir for 30 seconds to disperse the oil evenly. At 2% of the base weight the lavender scent is present but not sharp; if you want a stronger bar, add up to 3% (15 ml).

Pour the scented base slowly into the mould. Hold the jug close to the mould surface so the pour is short and doesn't trap air. If any bubbles appear on the surface, spritz lightly with a fine mist of isopropyl alcohol from a small spray bottle; the alcohol breaks the surface tension and the bubbles pop. Leave the mould undisturbed on a flat surface at room temperature for 2 hours.

The base sets from the surface inward. After 2 hours the bars should be firm and fully opaque or fully clear depending on the base type. Unmould by flexing the silicone mould or inverting the tin onto a board. If the bars release cleanly and the undersides feel solid, they are ready to use or wrap.

Patch-test a new bar on the inside of the forearm and leave 24-48 hours before regular use. Lavender essential oil at 2% is well within the dermal-use limit set by the IFRA guidelines for rinse-off soap, but individual sensitivities vary. The glycerin base itself is hypoallergenic at normal use concentrations.

Melt-and-pour bars keep 12 months in a cool dry cupboard, 2 years if individually wrapped in greaseproof or waxed paper. Glycerin is a humectant; an unwrapped bar in a steamy bathroom will bead surface moisture within a few weeks. This is cosmetically harmless but makes the bar slippery. Wrap any bar not in daily use. The spoilage cue to watch for is a rancid or paint-like smell, which signals the glycerin base has oxidised; discard that bar.
